metadata.summary: Every code repo must install a pre-commit framework (pre-commit, lefthook, or husky) blocking format/lint/secret/type failures before the commit object exists; agents never bypass with --no-verify.

content.title: Pre-Commit Hooks as the Non-Negotiable Merge Floor
content.summary: Every repository with code MUST install a pre-commit framework that runs format, lint, secret-scan and type-check hooks before a commit is created — never afterward. The framework is pre-commit (Python, broadest plug-in ecosystem) for polyglot repositories, lefthook (Go, parallel) for monorepos with throughput pressure, or husky (Node) for pure JavaScript stacks. AI agents NEVER bypass with --no-verify; on any hook failure they read the output, fix the root cause, and re-stage. The list of installed hooks is documented in the repository AGENTS.md so the agent sees the contract before it writes the first line.

why.p: Pre-commit hooks are the cheapest deterministic gate available: they run on the developer's machine in sub-second time on a diff, turning "did you remember to lint?" into a structural impossibility instead of a discipline question. Lefthook is roughly ten times faster than husky for the same hook set because it is a single Go binary running hooks in parallel, and pre-commit (pre-commit.com) ships hundreds of pre-built hooks (ruff, black, gitleaks, detect-secrets, eslint, prettier, terraform fmt, shellcheck) with isolated environments per hook so adding a check is a one-line YAML edit. Skipping the hook with --no-verify is the dominant agent failure mode, because it breaks the entire downstream chain (CI, reviewers, autogenerated changelogs).

list.item: Every repository that contains code, including throwaway prototypes that may grow.
Polyglot repositories where multiple language linters need consistent invocation (pre-commit or lefthook).
Monorepos with parallel hook execution requirements (lefthook).
Teams using AI coding agents — the hook list is the agent's compile-time contract.
Single-file gist repositories with no merge workflow — overhead exceeds the benefit.
Read-only or archived repositories — no commits arrive that the hook could gate.
Documentation-only repositories with no executable artifacts — a markdownlint hook still helps but the framework choice is overkill if there is one file.

section.name: hook-floor
section.p: A pre-commit framework runs on git commit and aborts the commit if any registered hook returns non-zero. Because the abort happens before the commit object is written, the failing diff never enters local history and never reaches CI. AI agents observe the failure as a tool error and must read the output and patch the underlying defect, not bypass the gate.

rule.statement: Every repository with code MUST register a pre-commit framework (pre-commit, lefthook, or husky) with hooks for at minimum: formatter, linter, secret scan, and (where applicable) type-check. Agents MUST NOT pass --no-verify or core.hooksPath=/dev/null; on any hook failure they MUST diagnose the failure, fix the offending change, re-stage the file, and re-run the commit.
rule.rationale: The hook is the cheapest deterministic gate (sub-second on a diff) and runs before the commit object exists, so a bypassed hook silently disables every downstream check that trusts the local history. Agents that learn --no-verify as a recovery strategy regress the entire team's quality floor; the rule is structural, not stylistic.

example.code: # agent run after a hook failure $ git commit -m "feat: add user import" ruff.....................................................................Failed - hook id: ruff - exit code: 1 src/users.py:12:1: F401 unused import $ ruff check --fix src/users.py $ git add src/users.py $ git commit -m "feat: add user import"
example.note: Agent reads the failure, runs the autofixer, re-stages, and commits — the hook is the contract.

example.code: $ git commit -m "feat: add user import" --no-verify [main abc1234] feat: add user import
example.note: Bypass succeeds locally but pushes a broken file into the chain; CI may catch it but the local gate is now untrusted.

antipattern.description: Treating the hook as advisory and adding it only on the developer's machine, not in the pinned config in the repository.
antipattern.reason: A hook that is not in .pre-commit-config.yaml (or lefthook.yml) is invisible to teammates and to agents — the contract collapses to a single human's habits.

section.name: framework-choice
section.p: The three mainstream frameworks solve overlapping but not identical problems. Stacking two of them (for example husky for JS hooks plus pre-commit for Python hooks) doubles maintenance and creates ordering ambiguity when both fire on the same commit.

rule.statement: Each repository MUST select exactly one pre-commit framework based on the rule: polyglot or Python-heavy → pre-commit; monorepo or throughput-sensitive → lefthook; pure Node + npm scripts → husky. The chosen config file (.pre-commit-config.yaml, lefthook.yml, or .husky/pre-commit) MUST be committed at the repository root.
rule.rationale: A single framework yields a single ordering, a single bootstrap step, and a single place to read the hook contract. lefthook's parallel execution is meaningfully faster (roughly 10x reported) when more than three hooks run, but pre-commit's plug-in ecosystem is the broadest, so the choice is driven by repository shape, not preference.

example.code: # lefthook.yml — parallel hooks for a monorepo pre-commit: parallel: true commands: ruff: glob: "*.py" run: uv run ruff check --fix {staged_files} biome: glob: "*.{ts,tsx,js,jsx}" run: pnpm biome check --write {staged_files} gitleaks: run: gitleaks protect --staged --redact
example.note: Three hooks run concurrently; each sees only the files it owns via glob.

example.code: # both frameworks installed in the same repo .husky/pre-commit # runs eslint .pre-commit-config.yaml # also runs eslint
example.note: Two frameworks compete for the same commit lifecycle; eslint runs twice and ordering with secret scanning becomes undefined.

antipattern.description: Choosing husky for a polyglot Python+TS+Terraform repo because the lead developer "knows npm".
antipattern.reason: husky has no Python isolation and no Terraform plug-ins; you end up shelling out to global tools that drift between machines, defeating the gate's determinism.

template.purpose: Minimal polyglot pre-commit config with ruff, gitleaks, prettier and a typecheck stage.
Equivalent lefthook config showing parallel execution and stage selection.

see-also.link: pre-commit.com — the canonical polyglot pre-commit framework.
husky vs lefthook vs lint-staged — 2026 comparison.
